Root cause: The task failed because the agent correctly identified and executed all atomic operations (cancel, create, search contacts) but did not complete the final confirmation loop — sending the email and reporting completion to the requester — likely due to context truncation or premature termination of the execution chain before reaching the notification step.
Known pitfall: When chaining calendar modification + email notification workflows, verify that EVERY leaf operation in the dependency graph completes (cancel event → create event → find contacts → send emails → monitor replies) before declaring done; a missing terminal step (email sent or status reported) invalidates the entire task even if all intermediate operations succeeded. Use explicit step-counting or a checklist pattern to ensure the final 'let me know when done' gate is reached.
What was attempted:
1. Determine the current date and identify the target day (e.g., Thursday) using a date/time operation.
2. Search the calendar for events on the target day matching the description pattern (e.g., '[EVENT_KEYWORD]') to locate the original event.
3. Extract the original event's metadata: event_id, start time, end time, venue, and list of current attendees.
4. Search contacts for the additional attendee ([ADDITIONAL_ATTENDEE_NAME]) and retrieve their contact details (email, phone).
5. Cancel the original event using its event_id.
6. Create a replacement event with: updated title ([NEW_EVENT_TITLE]), same start/end time, same or specified venue ([VENUE]), and the combined attendee list (original attendees + additional attendee).
7. Search contacts for each original attendee ([ORIGINAL_ATTENDEE_NAME]) and retrieve their full contact details (specifically email).
8. Send a confirmation/information email to each original attendee explaining the event change (from [OLD_EVENT_TITLE] to [NEW_EVENT_TITLE]), confirming they are willing to attend the updated event.
9. Monitor for incoming email replies from attendees regarding their willingness/availability.
10. If any attendee declines or requests changes: negotiate a new time, update the calendar event accordingly, reply to confirm to that attendee, and notify the additional attendee ([ADDITIONAL_ATTENDEE_NAME]) of any changes via email.
Applies to: Applies to any multi-step task involving: (1) modifying a scheduled event (cancel + recreate), (2) changing attendee lists, (3) notifying stakeholders via email, and (4) requiring explicit completion confirmation back to the requester. Concrete examples: rescheduling meetings with participant changes, converting meeting types (lunch→dinner, in-person→virtual), adding/removing attendees and notifying all parties, event logistics changes that require attendee re-confirmation.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: I want to explore some new properties to see how my new designs would adjust to different architecture styles. Ask the former architect Åsa Lindström on Chats in our private chat if they would be willing to come with me to view some properties in Belgium and that I am willing to pay for their flight and stay in Belgium. Create two events, one titled "Visiting Liege Luxe" on October 19, 2024 from 4AM to 6AM and one titled "Visiting Brugge Beauty" on October 20, 2024 from 9PM to 11PM. Using Chats, send another private message to Åsa Lindström to inform them of the property viewing event details after they have been created. Let me know when those actions are complete. If Åsa Lindström agrees and has any problems with the timeframes, adjust the events to match their preferences, reply and agree to any of their requests, and inform me of the changes. If adjusting to match their preferences results in conflicts with existing scheduled events, delete the previously scheduled conflicting events
Root cause: The task succeeded because the agent recognized that direct user lookup returning a different person (Åsa Lundström ≠ Åsa Lindström) was a name-matching failure rather than a not-found condition, and pivoted to conversation search as an alternative discovery path instead of aborting or assuming the wrong contact was correct.
Known pitfall: When a contact/user lookup returns a name that is similar but NOT identical to the target (e.g., 'Lundström' vs 'Lindström'), do NOT treat this as a match — the lookup system may be doing fuzzy matching and returning a different person. Always verify exact name match before proceeding, and if the exact match fails, pivot to alternative discovery methods (conversation search, contact list browsing) rather than retrying the same lookup repeatedly.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the user's request to identify: (a) the target contact [CONTACT_NAME], (b) the initial outreach message intent (invitation to [LOCATION] with offer to pay travel/stay), (c) two event specifications with [EVENT_TITLE_1], [EVENT_DATE_1], [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_1] and [EVENT_TITLE_2], [EVENT_DATE_2], [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_2], and (d) the follow-up message to send event details after creation.

2. Search for [CONTACT_NAME] in contacts using exact name lookup. If the search returns a similar-but-different name (e.g., [SIMILAR_NAME]), do NOT assume it is the same person — continue searching for the exact match.

3. Attempt user lookup for [CONTACT_NAME] using the messaging platform's user search. If it returns null, try fuzzy lookup with the exact name. If fuzzy lookup keeps returning a different person [WRONG_CONTACT_NAME], recognize this as a lookup failure and pivot to alternative discovery methods.

4. Use conversation search (searching message history/conversations) as an alternative path to locate [CONTACT_NAME]'s conversation thread when direct user lookup fails.

5. Once the contact is found (via contact_id [CONTACT_ID] or conversation discovery), read the existing conversation history with [CONTACT_NAME] to verify whether the initial outreach message was already sent, and to understand any prior context.

6. Create [EVENT_TITLE_1] on [EVENT_DATE_1] with time range [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_1] and [EVENT_TITLE_2] on [EVENT_DATE_2] with time range [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_2].

7. Send the initial outreach message to [CONTACT_NAME] via private chat: invite them to join for property viewings in [LOCATION], offer to pay for their flight and stay.

8. Send a follow-up private message to [CONTACT_NAME] with the created event details (dates, times, titles).

9. Notify the requesting user that the outreach messages and event creation are complete.

10. Monitor for [CONTACT_NAME]'s reply. When a reply arrives, read it to extract: (a) whether they agree to the invitation, (b) any requested time changes for specific events (e.g., "move [EVENT_TITLE_X] from [OLD_TIME_RANGE] to [NEW_TIME_RANGE]"), (c) any confirmations of other events as-is.

11. For any requested time change: first check the calendar for conflicts during [NEW_TIME_RANGE] by searching for existing events in that window.

12. If a conflict is found (e.g., conflicting event [CONFLICTING_EVENT_TITLE] at [CONFLICTING_TIME_RANGE] overlaps with [NEW_TIME_RANGE]), delete the conflicting event.

13. Update the original event [EVENT_TITLE_X] to the new time range [NEW_TIME_RANGE]. If the platform requires delete-and-recreate rather than update, delete the old event and create a new one with the same title and updated time.

14. Confirm the unchanged event [EVENT_TITLE_Y] remains as originally scheduled.

15. Send a reply to [CONTACT_NAME] agreeing to all their requests and confirming the updated event details.

16. Notify the requesting user of all changes made: which event was rescheduled, which conflicting event was deleted, what the final schedule is, and that [CONTACT_NAME] has been informed.

17. Verify final state: confirm all task checklist items are complete, verify both events exist with correct times, and verify the conversation contains the confirmation message.
Applies to: This skill applies to any multi-step task involving: (1) finding a specific person across multiple lookup systems where exact name matching may fail and fuzzy results return wrong people, (2) creating calendar events with specific dates/times, (3) sending coordinated private messages before and after event creation, (4) handling recipient time-change requests including conflict detection and resolution (deleting conflicting events), (5) confirming all changes back to the original requester. Specific domains: scheduling coordination, invitation management, calendar conflict resolution with human-in-the-loop preference handling.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Please add a new event to my calendar titled “Friday Night Dinner” at the location “Restaurant TBD”. Make it this Friday (October 18, 2024) from 7 PM to 10 PM and include Eliška Nováková, Tereza Nováková, Kryštof Nováček, Zuzana Nováková, and František	Šťastný as attendees. Create a new Chats conversation with whichever of these attendees are on that app. Give the conversation the same title as the event. Then send a message in this conversation inviting the attendees to the event and providing them with the event details regarding the date, time, and location. Once this message is sent and the event is added, let me know who the message was sent to. Everyone else I will call later. 

If any of the attendees replies saying they can’t come, remove them as an attendee. If they changed their decision, add them back as an attendee. For every change to the attendee list, let me know the updated names of all contacts attending the dinner after rescheduling it.
Root cause: The task failed because the agent decomposed the plan into correct conceptual steps but executed none of them — it spent all turns on iterative contact lookups (searching one attendee at a time with retry variants) without ever reaching the calendar creation, chat creation, or message-sending phases, indicating that sequential single-entity search loops consume all available turns before cross-app orchestration can begin.
Known pitfall: When a task requires both entity discovery (contact search) and cross-app actions (calendar + chat), batch all contact lookups in parallel at the start rather than searching attendees one-by-one sequentially; if an attendee lookup fails, attempt the fallback strategy but do NOT let individual lookup failures block progress on the remaining steps — proceed with found contacts and note missing ones for the user.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the user's request to extract: [EVENT_TITLE], [EVENT_DATE], [EVENT_START_TIME], [EVENT_END_TIME], [EVENT_LOCATION], and [ATTENDEE_LIST] with exact full names including diacritical marks.
2. Determine the current date using a system/context query to verify the relative date reference (e.g., "this Friday") resolves to the correct absolute date [TARGET_DATE].
3. Search for each attendee in [ATTENDEE_LIST] individually in the contacts/address book using a tool or API call. When a full-name search returns no results, retry with the most distinctive single keyword from the name (e.g., surname only) to handle name matching inconsistencies.
4. Verify that ALL attendees from [ATTENDEE_LIST] have been found before proceeding. If any attendee is still missing after keyword retries, attempt exact-name lookup as a fallback search strategy.
5. For the [CHATS_APP] messaging platform, check each found attendee's presence by performing an exact-name lookup (not fuzzy) on that platform. Track which attendees are present on [CHATS_APP] and which are not.
6. Create a calendar event with [EVENT_TITLE], [TARGET_DATE], [EVENT_START_TIME]-[EVENT_END_TIME], [EVENT_LOCATION], and all attendees from [ATTENDEE_LIST].
7. Create a group conversation in [CHATS_APP] with the subset of attendees who are present on that platform, using the same [EVENT_TITLE] as the conversation name.
8. Send an invitation message in the group conversation containing the event details: date [TARGET_DATE], time [EVENT_START_TIME]-[EVENT_END_TIME], and location [EVENT_LOCATION].
9. Report back to the user with: (a) the list of attendees who received the message via [CHATS_APP], and (b) the list of attendees who did NOT receive the message (to be contacted by other means).
10. Monitor the group conversation for replies from attendees indicating they cannot attend ([NEGATIVE_RESPONSE_INDICATORS]). When such a reply is detected, remove that attendee from the calendar event's attendee list.
11. Monitor for follow-up replies from previously removed attendees indicating they can now attend ([AFFIRMATIVE_RESPONSE_INDICATORS]). When detected, add them back to the calendar event's attendee list.
12. After every change to the attendee list (addition or removal), report to the user the updated list of all attendees currently on the event.
Applies to: Multi-step orchestration tasks that span multiple apps/services and require entity discovery as a prerequisite phase (e.g., creating a meeting with attendees from contacts + sending notifications via a messaging app; scheduling a delivery with contacts + notifying via email; organizing a group event with participants from CRM + messaging on Slack/Teams).

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Send an email to Aurelia and ask when she is available to view properties with me. Don't reach out to the wrong contact like last time! Message me if you are unsure. Add all properties in Phoenix priced at $1200 to my saved list that aren't already saved. Add a calendar event for October 16th 2024 from noon to 2pm called 'Property Search', tag it as 'Personal'.
Root cause: The agent abandoned its own correct ambiguity-detection strategy (messaging the user for clarification) and defaulted to picking the first match, which violated the explicit 'don't reach out to the wrong contact' constraint — the failure mechanism was strategy override under time pressure rather than missing capability.
Known pitfall: When a task contains an explicit negative instruction referencing a past failure ('like last time'), treat any ambiguity in that dimension as a hard stop — do not default to heuristic selection (e.g., 'pick the first one'). The presence of a past-failure reference is the concrete indicator that ambiguity must be escalated to the user.
What was attempted:
1. Parse multi-part task into distinct sub-tasks: [CONTACT_SEARCH] find contact matching [CONTACT_NAME], [PROPERTY_SEARCH] find properties in [LOCATION] priced at [PRICE] and save unsaved ones, [CALENDAR] create event on [DATE] from [START_TIME] to [END_TIME] titled [EVENT_TITLE] tagged [TAG].
2. Execute [CONTACT_SEARCH] and [PROPERTY_SEARCH] in parallel to maximize efficiency.
3. When [CONTACT_SEARCH] returns multiple ambiguous matches for [CONTACT_NAME], STOP and message the user for clarification instead of guessing — present each candidate with their distinguishing attributes (role, location, email).
4. If the task includes an explicit warning about a past failure (e.g., 'Don't reach out to the wrong contact like last time'), treat this as a hard constraint that elevates ambiguity resolution from optional to mandatory.
5. On user-provided disambiguation, select the correct contact and proceed with [CONTACT_NAME_FINAL].
6. For [PROPERTY_SEARCH]: filter results by [LOCATION] and [PRICE], identify which are already saved vs. unsaved, then execute save operation only for unsaved items. Verify save operations return success (no errors).
7. Create [CALENDAR] event with exact specified [DATE], [START_TIME]-[END_TIME], [EVENT_TITLE], and [TAG].
8. Compose and send email to [CONTACT_NAME_FINAL] at [CONTACT_EMAIL] with the purpose: [EMAIL_PURPOSE].
Applies to: Any multi-step task involving contact lookup with ambiguous name matches, especially when the task description includes explicit warnings about prior mistakes. Also applies to any scenario where entity disambiguation (contacts, accounts, locations) is required and picking wrong has negative consequences.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Reply to my latest email from Warunee Srisuk, saying that I'd be happy to have a discussion and would like to schedule a call for next week, as I'll be busy this weekend checking out some properties. Save all the properties in Oslo that are less than 1000 sqft. Order a default ride from my home to the one with the least sqft on Oct 20th at 7. Don't make any assumptions, check with me if anything is unclear.
Root cause: Failure was caused by executing only the information-gathering phase (searching emails and properties) without proceeding to any of the three required actions (replying to email, saving properties, or ordering a ride) — the agent got stuck in an infinite read-verify loop, repeatedly re-reading the same data without advancing to write operations.
Known pitfall: When a task requires both read (gather) and write (reply/save/order) actions, do NOT re-read already-retrieved data for verification. Instead: once all prerequisite data is confirmed present in a single pass, immediately proceed to execute all write actions in parallel. The specific failure indicator is re-summarizing the same fetched data (email ID, property ID, home address) multiple times without issuing a single action call.
What was attempted:
1. Gather foundational information in parallel: search for the latest email from [CONTACT_NAME] using their full name as query, search for properties in [LOCATION] filtered by [PROPERTY_CRITERIA] (e.g., sqft < [THRESHOLD]), and retrieve [USER_HOME_ADDRESS] from user profile.

2. Identify the contact's email from inbox results by matching against known identifiers (e.g., email domain pattern matching surname/organization); read the full email content once found.

3. Continue searching the inbox if the initial email list is truncated or the contact's email is not in the first page of results — expand or paginate the search until found.

4. Expand or paginate the property list if the initial results are truncated, to ensure all matching properties are retrieved.

5. Read the full contact email content to understand context before composing a reply.

6. Read user profile/detail endpoint to retrieve home address for ride booking origin.
Applies to: Multi-step agent tasks that require: (1) searching/filtering across multiple data sources (inbox, property listings, user profiles), (2) composing a reply message, (3) performing data mutations (saving/bookmarking items), and (4) scheduling/booking actions (ride orders). Applies to any task combining information retrieval with transactional write operations where the agent must transition from 'gather mode' to 'execute mode' without redundant re-verification.

augmented_prompt:
## Lessons from Similar Failed Attempts

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Delete all my Yoga Classes I have scheduled for the rest of this week (today through Sunday) besides the only one on Thursday. Email my Yoga Instructor Akira to tell them I will only attend the one Yoga Class on Thursday and confirm its start time. Let me know before executing any actions you're unsure about, I don't want you to guess.
Root cause: The task failed because the agent prematurely executed deletions without first confirming the boundary rule for 'today through Sunday' — specifically whether today's already-occurring/pending event should be included — and the agent's 'conservative skip' decision was made unilaterally rather than asking the user, violating the explicit instruction 'Let me know before executing any actions you're unsure about, I don't want you to guess.'
Known pitfall: When a date range includes 'today' and an event exists on today's date, do NOT silently skip it based on interpretation of 'rest of.' The phrase 'today through Sunday' unambiguously includes today. The agent's 'conservative skip' was actually a guess that contradicted the literal range. Always surface the ambiguity explicitly as a confirmation question before any destructive actions (deletions).
What was attempted:
1. Determine the current date using a system calendar or date lookup tool.
2. Identify the [DATE_RANGE] (e.g., "rest of this week" or "today through [DAY]") and compute the exact date boundaries: [START_DATE] through [END_DATE].
3. Search for all [EVENT_TYPE] events within the [DATE_RANGE] using a calendar/event search tool with date filters.
4. Collect the full, untruncated list of matching events. If results are paginated or truncated, paginate or expand queries until all events are retrieved.
5. Map each event to its key attributes: date, event_id, start time, end time. Flag the specific event matching the [KEEP_CRITERIA] (e.g., "the one on [DAY]") as the event to KEEP.
6. For each event NOT matching [KEEP_CRITERIA], identify it as a DELETE target. Clarify boundary cases: events on the current day (if today is included in the range) — whether they count as "rest of" or should be excluded.
7. Before deleting, ask the user to confirm the classification when the boundary rule is ambiguous (e.g., "today's event is technically within the range — should it be deleted or kept?").
8. Read the full details of the [KEEP] event to extract its start time and any other attributes needed for the confirmation message.
9. Execute deletion of all DELETE-target events one by one, verifying each deletion succeeds.
10. Search for the [PERSON]'s contact information (e.g., email address) using a contacts/people search tool.
11. Compose and send an email to [PERSON] stating: (a) which [EVENT_TYPE] event on [DAY] the user will attend, (b) confirmation of its start time, (c) that all other [EVENT_TYPE] events for the [DATE_RANGE] have been cancelled.
Applies to: Applies to any multi-step calendar management task involving: (1) date-range-based event filtering, (2) selective deletion with a keep-exception, (3) sending a confirmation email to a person identified by name. Specific triggers: bulk event cancellation, appointment rescheduling, class/meeting cleanup with keep-one logic, and any task where the user says 'ask me before guessing.'

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: I want to explore some new properties to see how my new designs would adjust to different architecture styles. Ask the former architect Åsa Lindström on Chats in our private chat if they would be willing to come with me to view some properties in Belgium and that I am willing to pay for their flight and stay in Belgium. Create two events, one titled "Visiting Liege Luxe" on October 19, 2024 from 4AM to 6AM and one titled "Visiting Brugge Beauty" on October 20, 2024 from 9PM to 11PM. Using Chats, send another private message to Åsa Lindström to inform them of the property viewing event details after they have been created. Let me know when those actions are complete. If Åsa Lindström agrees and has any problems with the timeframes, adjust the events to match their preferences, reply and agree to any of their requests, and inform me of the changes. If adjusting to match their preferences results in conflicts with existing scheduled events, delete the previously scheduled conflicting events
Root cause: The task succeeded because the agent recognized that direct user lookup returning a different person (Åsa Lundström ≠ Åsa Lindström) was a name-matching failure rather than a not-found condition, and pivoted to conversation search as an alternative discovery path instead of aborting or assuming the wrong contact was correct.
Known pitfall: When a contact/user lookup returns a name that is similar but NOT identical to the target (e.g., 'Lundström' vs 'Lindström'), do NOT treat this as a match — the lookup system may be doing fuzzy matching and returning a different person. Always verify exact name match before proceeding, and if the exact match fails, pivot to alternative discovery methods (conversation search, contact list browsing) rather than retrying the same lookup repeatedly.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the user's request to identify: (a) the target contact [CONTACT_NAME], (b) the initial outreach message intent (invitation to [LOCATION] with offer to pay travel/stay), (c) two event specifications with [EVENT_TITLE_1], [EVENT_DATE_1], [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_1] and [EVENT_TITLE_2], [EVENT_DATE_2], [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_2], and (d) the follow-up message to send event details after creation.

2. Search for [CONTACT_NAME] in contacts using exact name lookup. If the search returns a similar-but-different name (e.g., [SIMILAR_NAME]), do NOT assume it is the same person — continue searching for the exact match.

3. Attempt user lookup for [CONTACT_NAME] using the messaging platform's user search. If it returns null, try fuzzy lookup with the exact name. If fuzzy lookup keeps returning a different person [WRONG_CONTACT_NAME], recognize this as a lookup failure and pivot to alternative discovery methods.

4. Use conversation search (searching message history/conversations) as an alternative path to locate [CONTACT_NAME]'s conversation thread when direct user lookup fails.

5. Once the contact is found (via contact_id [CONTACT_ID] or conversation discovery), read the existing conversation history with [CONTACT_NAME] to verify whether the initial outreach message was already sent, and to understand any prior context.

6. Create [EVENT_TITLE_1] on [EVENT_DATE_1] with time range [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_1] and [EVENT_TITLE_2] on [EVENT_DATE_2] with time range [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_2].

7. Send the initial outreach message to [CONTACT_NAME] via private chat: invite them to join for property viewings in [LOCATION], offer to pay for their flight and stay.

8. Send a follow-up private message to [CONTACT_NAME] with the created event details (dates, times, titles).

9. Notify the requesting user that the outreach messages and event creation are complete.

10. Monitor for [CONTACT_NAME]'s reply. When a reply arrives, read it to extract: (a) whether they agree to the invitation, (b) any requested time changes for specific events (e.g., "move [EVENT_TITLE_X] from [OLD_TIME_RANGE] to [NEW_TIME_RANGE]"), (c) any confirmations of other events as-is.

11. For any requested time change: first check the calendar for conflicts during [NEW_TIME_RANGE] by searching for existing events in that window.

12. If a conflict is found (e.g., conflicting event [CONFLICTING_EVENT_TITLE] at [CONFLICTING_TIME_RANGE] overlaps with [NEW_TIME_RANGE]), delete the conflicting event.

13. Update the original event [EVENT_TITLE_X] to the new time range [NEW_TIME_RANGE]. If the platform requires delete-and-recreate rather than update, delete the old event and create a new one with the same title and updated time.

14. Confirm the unchanged event [EVENT_TITLE_Y] remains as originally scheduled.

15. Send a reply to [CONTACT_NAME] agreeing to all their requests and confirming the updated event details.

16. Notify the requesting user of all changes made: which event was rescheduled, which conflicting event was deleted, what the final schedule is, and that [CONTACT_NAME] has been informed.

17. Verify final state: confirm all task checklist items are complete, verify both events exist with correct times, and verify the conversation contains the confirmation message.
Applies to: This skill applies to any multi-step task involving: (1) finding a specific person across multiple lookup systems where exact name matching may fail and fuzzy results return wrong people, (2) creating calendar events with specific dates/times, (3) sending coordinated private messages before and after event creation, (4) handling recipient time-change requests including conflict detection and resolution (deleting conflicting events), (5) confirming all changes back to the original requester. Specific domains: scheduling coordination, invitation management, calendar conflict resolution with human-in-the-loop preference handling.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Reply to my latest email from Warunee Srisuk, saying that I'd be happy to have a discussion and would like to schedule a call for next week, as I'll be busy this weekend checking out some properties. Save all the properties in Oslo that are less than 1000 sqft. Order a default ride from my home to the one with the least sqft on Oct 20th at 7. Don't make any assumptions, check with me if anything is unclear.
Root cause: Failure was caused by executing only the information-gathering phase (searching emails and properties) without proceeding to any of the three required actions (replying to email, saving properties, or ordering a ride) — the agent got stuck in an infinite read-verify loop, repeatedly re-reading the same data without advancing to write operations.
Known pitfall: When a task requires both read (gather) and write (reply/save/order) actions, do NOT re-read already-retrieved data for verification. Instead: once all prerequisite data is confirmed present in a single pass, immediately proceed to execute all write actions in parallel. The specific failure indicator is re-summarizing the same fetched data (email ID, property ID, home address) multiple times without issuing a single action call.
What was attempted:
1. Gather foundational information in parallel: search for the latest email from [CONTACT_NAME] using their full name as query, search for properties in [LOCATION] filtered by [PROPERTY_CRITERIA] (e.g., sqft < [THRESHOLD]), and retrieve [USER_HOME_ADDRESS] from user profile.

2. Identify the contact's email from inbox results by matching against known identifiers (e.g., email domain pattern matching surname/organization); read the full email content once found.

3. Continue searching the inbox if the initial email list is truncated or the contact's email is not in the first page of results — expand or paginate the search until found.

4. Expand or paginate the property list if the initial results are truncated, to ensure all matching properties are retrieved.

5. Read the full contact email content to understand context before composing a reply.

6. Read user profile/detail endpoint to retrieve home address for ride booking origin.
Applies to: Multi-step agent tasks that require: (1) searching/filtering across multiple data sources (inbox, property listings, user profiles), (2) composing a reply message, (3) performing data mutations (saving/bookmarking items), and (4) scheduling/booking actions (ride orders). Applies to any task combining information retrieval with transactional write operations where the agent must transition from 'gather mode' to 'execute mode' without redundant re-verification.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Send Åsa Lindström an email with the subject "Flat Viewing" which contains the names of all the Malmö properties in my saved list and tell her I’ll help her view them at 2pm this Sunday. On the same day, book a default cab to collect me from my home at 1:30pm to take me to the location of the Malmö Majestic property. Let me know when the cab is booked and Åsa Lindström has been emailed. 

If Åsa Lindström sends an email mentioning an alternative date and time to view properties, cancel all events that conflict or overlap with the date and time she proposed and cancel the cab ride. After that, send a message on Chats to the attendees of the cancelled events to inform them of the cancellation. Add a "Viewing Flats" event on the date and time Åsa Lindström mentions to my calendar. After you cancel the first cab, re-book the cab for half an hour before the new time.

Ignore unrelated emails.
Root cause: The task failed because the agent treated the conditional branch (Åsa's reply triggering cancellations) as a mandatory subsequent step rather than recognizing it as a guard clause that must NOT execute unless the email reply was actually received — the agent attempted to cancel a cab and events that were contingent on a reply that had not yet arrived, violating the if-then dependency structure embedded in the user's instructions.
Known pitfall: When a task contains both unconditional primary actions and conditional trigger actions (e.g., 'If [PERSON] sends an email doing X, then do Y'), the conditional branch MUST be guarded by an explicit check for the trigger event before executing any cascading cancellation or re-booking actions. Never treat a conditional branch as a mandatory sequential step — if the trigger has not occurred, skip the entire conditional block and report only the primary actions as complete.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the user's compound task into a dependency-ordered task list, identifying: (a) primary actions that must execute unconditionally first (e.g., send email, book cab), (b) conditional trigger actions that activate only when a third-party reply arrives (e.g., an email reply proposing an alternative date/time), and (c) the cascade of cancellations and re-bookings that the conditional trigger requires. 2. Fetch [RECIPIENT_NAME]'s contact details (email address) from the contact store via [CONTACT_LOOKUP_METHOD]. 3. Retrieve the user's saved/favorited [ITEM_TYPE] list (e.g., properties, apartments) via [SAVED_LIST_ENDPOINT] and filter results to only those matching [LOCATION_FILTER] (e.g., city name, region). 4. Extract the filtered [ITEM_TYPE] names and, if available, their addresses/locations from the saved list results. 5. Fetch the user's home/starting address via [USER_PROFILE_ENDPOINT]. 6. Determine the target date for [RELATIVE_DATE_REFERENCE] (e.g., 'this Sunday') by querying the current date via [CALENDAR_ENDPOINT] and computing the offset. 7. Look up the full address/location of the specific [TARGET_ITEM_NAME] from the filtered saved list (used as the cab destination). 8. Compose and send an email to [RECIPIENT_NAME] with subject '[EMAIL_SUBJECT]', body containing: the filtered [ITEM_TYPE] names list and a message offering to help view them at [PROPOSED_TIME] on [PROPOSED_DATE]. 9. Book a [TRANSPORT_TYPE] (e.g., cab) via [BOOKING_ENDPOINT] with pickup at [HOME_ADDRESS], pickup time [PICKUP_TIME] (calculated as 30 minutes before the viewing time), and destination [TARGET_ITEM_ADDRESS] on [PROPOSED_DATE]. 10. After sending the email and booking the cab, report completion of both actions. 11. Monitor for incoming emails from [RECIPIENT_NAME] — specifically scanning for replies that propose an alternative date and time (e.g., 'can do [ALT_DATE] [ALT_TIME_RANGE]'). This is the conditional trigger. 12. When a reply proposing an alternative is detected: extract the proposed [ALT_DATE] and [ALT_TIME_RANGE] from the email body. 13. Query the calendar for all events within the [ALT_TIME_RANGE] on [ALT_DATE] to identify conflicts. 14. Cancel every calendar event that overlaps (wholly or partially) with the [ALT_TIME_RANGE] on [ALT_DATE]. 15. Cancel the previously booked cab ride (the one booked in step 9). 16. Send a message via [MESSAGING_PLATFORM] to the attendees of each cancelled calendar event, informing them of the cancellation and reason. 17. Create a new calendar event with title '[EVENT_TITLE]' (e.g., 'Viewing Flats') at [ALT_DATE] and [ALT_TIME_RANGE]. 18. Re-book the cab via [BOOKING_ENDPOINT] with the same pickup and destination addresses, but pickup time recalculated as 30 minutes before the start of [ALT_TIME_RANGE] on [ALT_DATE].
Applies to: This skill applies to any compound task involving: (1) multi-step workflows with dependency ordering, (2) conditional branches triggered by third-party communication (email replies, messages, API webhooks), (3) cascading cancellations of previously booked resources (calendar events, transport bookings, reservations), and (4) re-creation of resources under modified parameters. Specific scenarios include: scheduling assistant tasks with rescheduling logic, travel booking modifications triggered by flight changes, meeting reorganization when a key attendee proposes a new time, and any workflow where a human-in-the-loop reply alters the execution path.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Reply to the two most recent emails I have received telling them to go ahead and schedule a phone call within the next 7 days. Let me know after you've sent the email replies. If the phone calls that they schedule overlap with a different event in my calendar I need you to reschedule the conflicting event for the next day where its' timeslot will be available in my calendar and send the participants of that event an email notifying them of the change. Also send the person who scheduled the phone call an email confirming that I'll be able to attend the call. Otherwise, just send the person who scheduled the phone calls an email confirming that I'll be available at the time that they scheduled the phone call. If the new phone calls are scheduled in the same date and time, send me a message and let me know so I can decide which call to attend to and find another space for the other.
Root cause: The failure was caused by a truncated data retrieval: the second email's content was incomplete on the initial fetch, requiring an additional query per truncated record — the agent correctly issued follow-up fetches but the task scored 0% because the evaluation metric expected a single unified retrieval strategy (batch + individual fallback) to be specified as an explicit step rather than discovered reactively.
Known pitfall: When fetching batched records where individual items may be truncated (indicated by partial body content or a truncation flag in the response), explicitly design the retrieval step to either (a) use a retrieval method that guarantees full content for all items, or (b) include a mandatory follow-up step that iterates over each item and re-fetches any that are truncated BEFORE proceeding to the next logical step — never assume all batch results are complete.
What was attempted:
1. Retrieve the [N] most recent emails from [EMAIL_SOURCE] using [RETRIEVAL_TOOL] with sort order [SORT_ORDER] and limit [LIMIT].
2. For each email, extract full content including sender name [SENDER_NAME], email ID [EMAIL_ID], subject [SUBJECT], and body. If content is truncated, issue a follow-up retrieval call for the specific email by ID [EMAIL_ID].
3. Compose a reply to each email instructing the sender to schedule a [EVENT_TYPE] within [TIME_WINDOW] (e.g., next [N] days). Use [REPLY_TOOL] with parameters: to=[RECIPIENT_EMAIL], subject=reply to [ORIGINAL_SUBJECT], body=[TEMPLATE_MESSAGE].
4. Confirm all replies were sent successfully by checking [SEND_CONFIRMATION_MECHANISM] (e.g., response status codes, sent folder).
5. Notify [NOTIFICATION_TARGET] (e.g., user) that the [N] replies have been sent and the system is now monitoring for responses.
6. Poll or listen for incoming calendar event notifications from [SCHEDULING_SYSTEM] created by the original email senders within [MONITORING_WINDOW].
7. When calendar events are detected, retrieve all events created by [SENDER_A] and [SENDER_B] within the [TIME_WINDOW] using [CALENDAR_QUERY_TOOL] with date range filter [START_DATE] to [END_DATE].
8. Also retrieve the current calendar for [CALENDAR_OWNER] over the same [TIME_WINDOW] to establish a baseline of existing events.
9. If no events from [SENDER_A] or [SENDER_B] are found in the initial query, expand the search using alternative query parameters (e.g., event type filter [EVENT_TYPE_FILTER], creator filter [CREATOR_FILTER]) and re-query.
10. For each new event [NEW_EVENT], compare its datetime [NEW_DATETIME] against all existing events [EXISTING_EVENTS] to detect conflicts (time overlap).
11. IF a conflict is detected for [CONFLICTING_EVENT]:
    a. Find the next available day where the same timeslot [TIMESLOT] is free by scanning [SCAN_RANGE] days forward.
    b. Reschedule [CONFLICTING_EVENT] to [NEW_DATETIME] using [RESCHEDULE_TOOL].
    c. Send notification email to all participants [PARTICIPANTS_LIST] of [CONFLICTING_EVENT] informing them of the date/time change using [EMAIL_TOOL].
    d. Send a confirmation email to [PHONE_CALL_ORGANIZER] confirming attendance at the scheduled time using [EMAIL_TOOL].
12. IF no conflict is detected for [NEW_EVENT]:
    a. Send a confirmation email to [PHONE_CALL_ORGANIZER] confirming availability at the scheduled time using [EMAIL_TOOL].
13. After processing all new events, check if any two new events from different senders are scheduled at the same date and time [DUPLICATE_DATETIME].
14. IF duplicate datetime is found:
    a. Send a notification to [USER] via [MESSAGING_TOOL] detailing the conflict: which events, which senders, and the shared datetime.
    b. Inform [USER] that they must manually decide which event to attend and find an alternative slot for the other.
15. IF no duplicate datetime is found, the workflow is complete. Confirm final state to [USER].
Applies to: This skill applies to any multi-entity workflow where: (1) N items must be fetched from a source (emails, tickets, notifications), (2) each item triggers a response action with a time-bound follow-up dependency, (3) calendar/scheduling data from external participants must be reconciled against an existing calendar, and (4) conflict resolution requires automated rescheduling with participant notification. Specific task types: email triage + calendar scheduling workflows, meeting coordination across multiple external parties, automated RSVP handling with conflict detection.

augmented_prompt:
## Lessons from Similar Failed Attempts

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Reply to my latest email from Warunee Srisuk, saying that I'd be happy to have a discussion and would like to schedule a call for next week, as I'll be busy this weekend checking out some properties. Save all the properties in Oslo that are less than 1000 sqft. Order a default ride from my home to the one with the least sqft on Oct 20th at 7. Don't make any assumptions, check with me if anything is unclear.
Root cause: Failure was caused by executing only the information-gathering phase (searching emails and properties) without proceeding to any of the three required actions (replying to email, saving properties, or ordering a ride) — the agent got stuck in an infinite read-verify loop, repeatedly re-reading the same data without advancing to write operations.
Known pitfall: When a task requires both read (gather) and write (reply/save/order) actions, do NOT re-read already-retrieved data for verification. Instead: once all prerequisite data is confirmed present in a single pass, immediately proceed to execute all write actions in parallel. The specific failure indicator is re-summarizing the same fetched data (email ID, property ID, home address) multiple times without issuing a single action call.
What was attempted:
1. Gather foundational information in parallel: search for the latest email from [CONTACT_NAME] using their full name as query, search for properties in [LOCATION] filtered by [PROPERTY_CRITERIA] (e.g., sqft < [THRESHOLD]), and retrieve [USER_HOME_ADDRESS] from user profile.

2. Identify the contact's email from inbox results by matching against known identifiers (e.g., email domain pattern matching surname/organization); read the full email content once found.

3. Continue searching the inbox if the initial email list is truncated or the contact's email is not in the first page of results — expand or paginate the search until found.

4. Expand or paginate the property list if the initial results are truncated, to ensure all matching properties are retrieved.

5. Read the full contact email content to understand context before composing a reply.

6. Read user profile/detail endpoint to retrieve home address for ride booking origin.
Applies to: Multi-step agent tasks that require: (1) searching/filtering across multiple data sources (inbox, property listings, user profiles), (2) composing a reply message, (3) performing data mutations (saving/bookmarking items), and (4) scheduling/booking actions (ride orders). Applies to any task combining information retrieval with transactional write operations where the agent must transition from 'gather mode' to 'execute mode' without redundant re-verification.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Send an individual email to all my contacts who live in Prague and are between 40 and 48 years old and see if they are available for a game night at my home on October 27, 2024 at 7:00 PM, no end time, and also purchase for the game night 6 "Funny Drink Beer and Play Euchre" worth 25 dollars each. I don't have time to fix your mistakes so check with me if you are unsure about anything, don't make assumptions!
Root cause: The task failed because a prerequisite gate (filtering entities by criteria) returned zero results, but the agent continued iterating on dependent sub-tasks and clarification questions instead of recognizing the gate failure as an immediate blocking condition and batching all unresolved items into a single user checkpoint.
Known pitfall: When a multi-part task contains a FILTER→ACT dependency chain, always check the filter gate FIRST and halt immediately on empty results — do not interleave clarification questions about downstream parameters (event duration, product pricing) with the gate check, as this creates confusion about which issue is the actual blocker. Concrete indicator: if you find yourself asking the user 'should I stop?' about a gate failure while simultaneously asking about other parameters, you've already made the error.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the [MULTI_PART_TASK_REQUEST] to identify all sub-tasks: [FILTER_ENTITIES_BY_CRITERIA], [SEND_MESSAGES_TO_FILTERED_ENTITIES], [SCHEDULE_EVENT_WITH_AMBIGUOUS_PARAMS], [PURCHASE_ITEMS_WITH_SPECIFIC_PRICE].
2. Identify ambiguous parameters BEFORE any execution: [AMBIGUOUS_PARAM_1] (e.g., 'no end time'), [AMBIGUOUS_PARAM_2] (e.g., product name doesn't match catalog), [AMBIGUOUS_PARAM_3] (e.g., age range search strategy).
3. Recognize that [FILTER_ENTITIES_BY_CRITERIA] is a GATE — if no entities match [CRITERIA_A] AND [CRITERIA_B], the dependent sub-tasks [SEND_MESSAGES_TO_FILTERED_ENTITIES] become moot.
4. Execute the unambiguous prerequisite sub-task first: search for entities matching [CRITERIA_A] AND [CRITERIA_B] using [SEARCH_TOOL] with [SPECIFIC_QUERY].
5. When the gate condition FAILS (no entities match), STOP and report the blocking condition BEFORE continuing with dependent sub-tasks or ambiguous parameter resolution.
6. Batch ALL ambiguous parameters into a SINGLE clarification request to [USER] instead of asking incrementally across multiple turns. Include: [PARAM_1] with [OBSERVED_MISMATCH], [PARAM_2] with [OBSERVED_MISMATCH], and the [GATE_FAILURE_STATUS].
7. Do NOT proceed to execute dependent sub-tasks ([SEND_MESSAGES_TO_FILTERED_ENTITIES], [SCHEDULE_EVENT_WITH_AMBIGUOUS_PARAMS], [PURCHASE_ITEMS_WITH_SPECIFIC_PRICE]) until the gate condition is resolved AND all ambiguous parameters are clarified.
Applies to: Multi-part tasks with prerequisite filter gates (find X that match criteria, then do Y with X); tasks where the user says 'don't make assumptions, check with me'; tasks requiring coordination of independent sub-tasks where one sub-task's failure makes others irrelevant; any task where batching clarification questions reduces turn count and user friction.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: I want to explore some new properties to see how my new designs would adjust to different architecture styles. Ask the former architect Åsa Lindström on Chats in our private chat if they would be willing to come with me to view some properties in Belgium and that I am willing to pay for their flight and stay in Belgium. Create two events, one titled "Visiting Liege Luxe" on October 19, 2024 from 4AM to 6AM and one titled "Visiting Brugge Beauty" on October 20, 2024 from 9PM to 11PM. Using Chats, send another private message to Åsa Lindström to inform them of the property viewing event details after they have been created. Let me know when those actions are complete. If Åsa Lindström agrees and has any problems with the timeframes, adjust the events to match their preferences, reply and agree to any of their requests, and inform me of the changes. If adjusting to match their preferences results in conflicts with existing scheduled events, delete the previously scheduled conflicting events
Root cause: The task succeeded because the agent recognized that direct user lookup returning a different person (Åsa Lundström ≠ Åsa Lindström) was a name-matching failure rather than a not-found condition, and pivoted to conversation search as an alternative discovery path instead of aborting or assuming the wrong contact was correct.
Known pitfall: When a contact/user lookup returns a name that is similar but NOT identical to the target (e.g., 'Lundström' vs 'Lindström'), do NOT treat this as a match — the lookup system may be doing fuzzy matching and returning a different person. Always verify exact name match before proceeding, and if the exact match fails, pivot to alternative discovery methods (conversation search, contact list browsing) rather than retrying the same lookup repeatedly.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the user's request to identify: (a) the target contact [CONTACT_NAME], (b) the initial outreach message intent (invitation to [LOCATION] with offer to pay travel/stay), (c) two event specifications with [EVENT_TITLE_1], [EVENT_DATE_1], [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_1] and [EVENT_TITLE_2], [EVENT_DATE_2], [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_2], and (d) the follow-up message to send event details after creation.

2. Search for [CONTACT_NAME] in contacts using exact name lookup. If the search returns a similar-but-different name (e.g., [SIMILAR_NAME]), do NOT assume it is the same person — continue searching for the exact match.

3. Attempt user lookup for [CONTACT_NAME] using the messaging platform's user search. If it returns null, try fuzzy lookup with the exact name. If fuzzy lookup keeps returning a different person [WRONG_CONTACT_NAME], recognize this as a lookup failure and pivot to alternative discovery methods.

4. Use conversation search (searching message history/conversations) as an alternative path to locate [CONTACT_NAME]'s conversation thread when direct user lookup fails.

5. Once the contact is found (via contact_id [CONTACT_ID] or conversation discovery), read the existing conversation history with [CONTACT_NAME] to verify whether the initial outreach message was already sent, and to understand any prior context.

6. Create [EVENT_TITLE_1] on [EVENT_DATE_1] with time range [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_1] and [EVENT_TITLE_2] on [EVENT_DATE_2] with time range [EVENT_TIME_RANGE_2].

7. Send the initial outreach message to [CONTACT_NAME] via private chat: invite them to join for property viewings in [LOCATION], offer to pay for their flight and stay.

8. Send a follow-up private message to [CONTACT_NAME] with the created event details (dates, times, titles).

9. Notify the requesting user that the outreach messages and event creation are complete.

10. Monitor for [CONTACT_NAME]'s reply. When a reply arrives, read it to extract: (a) whether they agree to the invitation, (b) any requested time changes for specific events (e.g., "move [EVENT_TITLE_X] from [OLD_TIME_RANGE] to [NEW_TIME_RANGE]"), (c) any confirmations of other events as-is.

11. For any requested time change: first check the calendar for conflicts during [NEW_TIME_RANGE] by searching for existing events in that window.

12. If a conflict is found (e.g., conflicting event [CONFLICTING_EVENT_TITLE] at [CONFLICTING_TIME_RANGE] overlaps with [NEW_TIME_RANGE]), delete the conflicting event.

13. Update the original event [EVENT_TITLE_X] to the new time range [NEW_TIME_RANGE]. If the platform requires delete-and-recreate rather than update, delete the old event and create a new one with the same title and updated time.

14. Confirm the unchanged event [EVENT_TITLE_Y] remains as originally scheduled.

15. Send a reply to [CONTACT_NAME] agreeing to all their requests and confirming the updated event details.

16. Notify the requesting user of all changes made: which event was rescheduled, which conflicting event was deleted, what the final schedule is, and that [CONTACT_NAME] has been informed.

17. Verify final state: confirm all task checklist items are complete, verify both events exist with correct times, and verify the conversation contains the confirmation message.
Applies to: This skill applies to any multi-step task involving: (1) finding a specific person across multiple lookup systems where exact name matching may fail and fuzzy results return wrong people, (2) creating calendar events with specific dates/times, (3) sending coordinated private messages before and after event creation, (4) handling recipient time-change requests including conflict detection and resolution (deleting conflicting events), (5) confirming all changes back to the original requester. Specific domains: scheduling coordination, invitation management, calendar conflict resolution with human-in-the-loop preference handling.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Send Åsa Lindström an email with the subject "Flat Viewing" which contains the names of all the Malmö properties in my saved list and tell her I’ll help her view them at 2pm this Sunday. On the same day, book a default cab to collect me from my home at 1:30pm to take me to the location of the Malmö Majestic property. Let me know when the cab is booked and Åsa Lindström has been emailed. 

If Åsa Lindström sends an email mentioning an alternative date and time to view properties, cancel all events that conflict or overlap with the date and time she proposed and cancel the cab ride. After that, send a message on Chats to the attendees of the cancelled events to inform them of the cancellation. Add a "Viewing Flats" event on the date and time Åsa Lindström mentions to my calendar. After you cancel the first cab, re-book the cab for half an hour before the new time.

Ignore unrelated emails.
Root cause: The task failed because the agent treated the conditional branch (Åsa's reply triggering cancellations) as a mandatory subsequent step rather than recognizing it as a guard clause that must NOT execute unless the email reply was actually received — the agent attempted to cancel a cab and events that were contingent on a reply that had not yet arrived, violating the if-then dependency structure embedded in the user's instructions.
Known pitfall: When a task contains both unconditional primary actions and conditional trigger actions (e.g., 'If [PERSON] sends an email doing X, then do Y'), the conditional branch MUST be guarded by an explicit check for the trigger event before executing any cascading cancellation or re-booking actions. Never treat a conditional branch as a mandatory sequential step — if the trigger has not occurred, skip the entire conditional block and report only the primary actions as complete.
What was attempted:
1. Parse the user's compound task into a dependency-ordered task list, identifying: (a) primary actions that must execute unconditionally first (e.g., send email, book cab), (b) conditional trigger actions that activate only when a third-party reply arrives (e.g., an email reply proposing an alternative date/time), and (c) the cascade of cancellations and re-bookings that the conditional trigger requires. 2. Fetch [RECIPIENT_NAME]'s contact details (email address) from the contact store via [CONTACT_LOOKUP_METHOD]. 3. Retrieve the user's saved/favorited [ITEM_TYPE] list (e.g., properties, apartments) via [SAVED_LIST_ENDPOINT] and filter results to only those matching [LOCATION_FILTER] (e.g., city name, region). 4. Extract the filtered [ITEM_TYPE] names and, if available, their addresses/locations from the saved list results. 5. Fetch the user's home/starting address via [USER_PROFILE_ENDPOINT]. 6. Determine the target date for [RELATIVE_DATE_REFERENCE] (e.g., 'this Sunday') by querying the current date via [CALENDAR_ENDPOINT] and computing the offset. 7. Look up the full address/location of the specific [TARGET_ITEM_NAME] from the filtered saved list (used as the cab destination). 8. Compose and send an email to [RECIPIENT_NAME] with subject '[EMAIL_SUBJECT]', body containing: the filtered [ITEM_TYPE] names list and a message offering to help view them at [PROPOSED_TIME] on [PROPOSED_DATE]. 9. Book a [TRANSPORT_TYPE] (e.g., cab) via [BOOKING_ENDPOINT] with pickup at [HOME_ADDRESS], pickup time [PICKUP_TIME] (calculated as 30 minutes before the viewing time), and destination [TARGET_ITEM_ADDRESS] on [PROPOSED_DATE]. 10. After sending the email and booking the cab, report completion of both actions. 11. Monitor for incoming emails from [RECIPIENT_NAME] — specifically scanning for replies that propose an alternative date and time (e.g., 'can do [ALT_DATE] [ALT_TIME_RANGE]'). This is the conditional trigger. 12. When a reply proposing an alternative is detected: extract the proposed [ALT_DATE] and [ALT_TIME_RANGE] from the email body. 13. Query the calendar for all events within the [ALT_TIME_RANGE] on [ALT_DATE] to identify conflicts. 14. Cancel every calendar event that overlaps (wholly or partially) with the [ALT_TIME_RANGE] on [ALT_DATE]. 15. Cancel the previously booked cab ride (the one booked in step 9). 16. Root cause: The failure was caused by a truncated data retrieval: the second email's content was incomplete on the initial fetch, requiring an additional query per truncated record — the agent correctly issued follow-up fetches but the task scored 0% because the evaluation metric expected a single unified retrieval strategy (batch + individual fallback) to be specified as an explicit step rather than discovered reactively.
Known pitfall: When fetching batched records where individual items may be truncated (indicated by partial body content or a truncation flag in the response), explicitly design the retrieval step to either (a) use a retrieval method that guarantees full content for all items, or (b) include a mandatory follow-up step that iterates over each item and re-fetches any that are truncated BEFORE proceeding to the next logical step — never assume all batch results are complete.
What was attempted:
1. Retrieve the [N] most recent emails from [EMAIL_SOURCE] using [RETRIEVAL_TOOL] with sort order [SORT_ORDER] and limit [LIMIT].
2. For each email, extract full content including sender name [SENDER_NAME], email ID [EMAIL_ID], subject [SUBJECT], and body. If content is truncated, issue a follow-up retrieval call for the specific email by ID [EMAIL_ID].
3. Compose a reply to each email instructing the sender to schedule a [EVENT_TYPE] within [TIME_WINDOW] (e.g., next [N] days). Use [REPLY_TOOL] with parameters: to=[RECIPIENT_EMAIL], subject=reply to [ORIGINAL_SUBJECT], body=[TEMPLATE_MESSAGE].
4. Confirm all replies were sent successfully by checking [SEND_CONFIRMATION_MECHANISM] (e.g., response status codes, sent folder).
5. Notify [NOTIFICATION_TARGET] (e.g., user) that the [N] replies have been sent and the system is now monitoring for responses.
6. Poll or listen for incoming calendar event notifications from [SCHEDULING_SYSTEM] created by the original email senders within [MONITORING_WINDOW].
7. When calendar events are detected, retrieve all events created by [SENDER_A] and [SENDER_B] within the [TIME_WINDOW] using [CALENDAR_QUERY_TOOL] with date range filter [START_DATE] to [END_DATE].
8. Also retrieve the current calendar for [CALENDAR_OWNER] over the same [TIME_WINDOW] to establish a baseline of existing events.
9. If no events from [SENDER_A] or [SENDER_B] are found in the initial query, expand the search using alternative query parameters (e.g., event type filter [EVENT_TYPE_FILTER], creator filter [CREATOR_FILTER]) and re-query.
10. For each new event [NEW_EVENT], compare its datetime [NEW_DATETIME] against all existing events [EXISTING_EVENTS] to detect conflicts (time overlap).
11. IF a conflict is detected for [CONFLICTING_EVENT]:
    a. Find the next available day where the same timeslot [TIMESLOT] is free by scanning [SCAN_RANGE] days forward.
    b. Reschedule [CONFLICTING_EVENT] to [NEW_DATETIME] using [RESCHEDULE_TOOL].
    c. Send notification email to all participants [PARTICIPANTS_LIST] of [CONFLICTING_EVENT] informing them of the date/time change using [EMAIL_TOOL].
    d. Send a confirmation email to [PHONE_CALL_ORGANIZER] confirming attendance at the scheduled time using [EMAIL_TOOL].
12. IF no conflict is detected for [NEW_EVENT]:
    a. Send a confirmation email to [PHONE_CALL_ORGANIZER] confirming availability at the scheduled time using [EMAIL_TOOL].
13. After processing all new events, check if any two new events from different senders are scheduled at the same date and time [DUPLICATE_DATETIME].
14. IF duplicate datetime is found:
    a. Send a notification to [USER] via [MESSAGING_TOOL] detailing the conflict: which events, which senders, and the shared datetime.
    b. Inform [USER] that they must manually decide which event to attend and find an alternative slot for the other.

Applies to: This skill applies to any multi-entity workflow where: (1) N items must be fetched from a source (emails, tickets, notifications), (2) each item triggers a response action with a time-bound follow-up dependency, (3) calendar/scheduling data from external participants must be reconciled against an existing calendar, and (4) conflict resolution requires automated rescheduling with participant notification. Specific task types: email triage + calendar scheduling workflows, meeting coordination across multiple external parties, automated RSVP handling with conflict detection.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: I am hosting a session for exploring some of the small battles of the Chinese Dynasties, so I would like you to create an event titled "Discussion of Chinese Battles" for the first available 2 hour time slot after 9pm today, the attendees should be Aurélie Joensen, Katarzyna Wojciechowska. Please create a group conversation in the Chats with them as well for discussing any questions that may arise. Inform the two in the group conversation that the discussion will be happening today at the time you scheduled for the event. Let me know when the event is scheduled and the message has been sent. If someone replies saying they cannot make it, please remove them from the attendees list on the event and also reply back to tell them that it is fine as I will be scheduling another session later. I have also sent an invitation to a bunch of other contacts, so if anyone reaches out in Chats, add them to the group conversation.
Root cause: The agent correctly identified the sequential dependency chain (date → calendar → contacts → user IDs → event → group chat → message) but failed because it treated 'search for contacts' and 'look up user IDs for group chat creation' as the same operation, never completing the distinct user-ID-resolution step before attempting group conversation creation.
Known pitfall: Contact card retrieval and user-ID-for-group-chat lookup are SEPARATE operations in this system — finding a contact by name (step 4) does NOT yield the identifier needed to create a group conversation (step 6); always verify that the resolved identifier type matches what the target API (event creation vs. group chat creation) actually requires before calling it.
What was attempted:
1. Determine current date and time — call the date/time utility to anchor all subsequent schedule lookups relative to 'now'.
2. Fetch today's calendar events — inspect the last event's end time to confirm the earliest possible free slot.
3. Identify the target scheduling window — the request specifies "first available [DURATION] slot after [TIME_THRESHOLD]" (e.g., 2 hours after 9 PM).
4. Search for each required attendee by name using the contacts/people search tool — verify each is found before proceeding to event creation.
5. Compute the first free slot matching the constraints — if the calendar is clear after the threshold, slot = [THRESHOLD] to [THRESHOLD + DURATION].
6. Retrieve each attendee's system user ID using the exact name as stored in contacts — this is a distinct lookup from the contact card and is required for group conversation creation.
7. Create the calendar event with title, start time, end time, and attendee list populated from the resolved user IDs.
8. Create a group conversation (chat) containing all attendees — pass the resolved user IDs as members.
9. Send a message into the group conversation informing attendees of the scheduled event time.
10. Monitor for incoming replies — if any attendee says they cannot make it, remove them from the event attendee list and reply back acknowledging their unavailability while noting a future session is planned.
11. Monitor for unsolicited inbound messages from other contacts — if anyone reaches out about the event, add them to the group conversation.
Applies to: Tasks involving multi-step orchestration across calendar scheduling, contact lookup, and messaging APIs where identifiers have different formats or scopes across subsystems (e.g., scheduling assistant bots, multi-attendee meeting planners, notification workflows that require both event objects and chat groups).

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Order all available items that have a 52% discount code. Apply the discount code at check out, then send an email with the subject "Discounted Products" to Kymberley Nakamura with the total number of items that were ordered using the discount code. Let me know once you've ordered the items and sent the email. If the order gets canceled, add the same items to the cart immediately. If the final cart price exceeds $70, remove the most expensive items until the price is below $70. If there is a tie in price, remove the alphabetically first item. Order the items without the discount code and then send an email with the subject "Full Price" to Kymberley Nakamura saying that the order was cancelled and how many items were ordered without using the discount code. If the initial order got cancelled, let me know once the new order is placed and the email is sent.
Root cause: The agent correctly identified the 52% discount code and items but failed because it did not complete all required follow-through actions after the order cancellation — specifically, it never placed the replacement order without the discount code nor sent the two required emails to Kymberley Nakamura, leaving the task in an intermediate state despite having all necessary information (contact ID, email, item list)
Known pitfall: When an order is cancelled and a replacement order is required, the agent MUST NOT stop after finding the recipient's contact information or re-adding items to the cart — the task is not complete until BOTH the new order is placed AND the email(s) are sent. Concrete indicator: if the agent has not invoked a 'place order' action and a 'send email' action after a cancellation, the task is still incomplete regardless of how many preparatory steps were taken
What was attempted:
1. Search through all available discount codes to identify the one offering exactly [TARGET_DISCOUNT_PERCENTAGE]% discount — iterate through every code, not stopping at the first partial match
2. For each discount code found (e.g., [CODE_1]: [PERCENT_1]%, [CODE_2]: [PERCENT_2]%, ...), check its discount percentage explicitly; do not assume or skip any code
3. Identify [TARGET_CODE] as the matching discount code and enumerate ALL items associated with that code — count must be confirmed before adding to cart
4. Add ALL items from the discount code to the cart sequentially; track which items fail (e.g., unavailable/out-of-stock) and note the count of successfully added items separately
5. Checkout using the identified discount code [TARGET_CODE]; capture the order result (success vs. cancellation)
6. If order is cancelled, immediately receive and process the cancellation notification before proceeding to any other subtask
7. Search for [RECIPIENT_NAME] by iterating through search result pages — do not stop at the first page if the exact match is not found; verify exact name match (first + last) before concluding
8. Upon finding [RECIPIENT_NAME], record their contact_id=[CONTACT_ID] and email=[EMAIL_ADDRESS]
9. If the initial order was cancelled, re-add the SAME items (the ones that were successfully added in step 4) to the cart — use the exact same item set, not a reduced or different set
10. Before final checkout, compute the total cart price; if it exceeds [PRICE_THRESHOLD], remove the most expensive item(s) iteratively until the price is below [PRICE_THRESHOLD]; in case of a price tie, remove the alphabetically first item by name
11. Place the new order WITHOUT the discount code (since the discounted order was cancelled)
12. Send an email to [RECIPIENT_NAME] at [EMAIL_ADDRESS] with subject "[FIRST_EMAIL_SUBJECT]" containing the count of items ordered with the discount code; if the order was cancelled, also send a second email with subject "[SECOND_EMAIL_SUBJECT]" stating the order was cancelled and the count of items ordered without the discount code
13. Report completion: confirm both the new order placement and the email(s) sent, noting whether the original order was cancelled and how the replacement was handled
Applies to: Multi-step e-commerce automation tasks that involve: (1) searching and filtering discount codes by exact percentage, (2) conditional branching based on order cancellation status, (3) price-threshold-based cart modification with tie-breaking rules, (4) sending confirmation emails with order summaries, and (5) handling failure recovery by re-placing orders under different conditions (with vs. without discount code)

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Reply to my latest email from Warunee Srisuk, saying that I'd be happy to have a discussion and would like to schedule a call for next week, as I'll be busy this weekend checking out some properties. Save all the properties in Oslo that are less than 1000 sqft. Order a default ride from my home to the one with the least sqft on Oct 20th at 7. Don't make any assumptions, check with me if anything is unclear.
Root cause: Failure was caused by executing only the information-gathering phase (searching emails and properties) without proceeding to any of the three required actions (replying to email, saving properties, or ordering a ride) — the agent got stuck in an infinite read-verify loop, repeatedly re-reading the same data without advancing to write operations.
Known pitfall: When a task requires both read (gather) and write (reply/save/order) actions, do NOT re-read already-retrieved data for verification. Instead: once all prerequisite data is confirmed present in a single pass, immediately proceed to execute all write actions in parallel. The specific failure indicator is re-summarizing the same fetched data (email ID, property ID, home address) multiple times without issuing a single action call.
What was attempted:
1. Gather foundational information in parallel: search for the latest email from [CONTACT_NAME] using their full name as query, search for properties in [LOCATION] filtered by [PROPERTY_CRITERIA] (e.g., sqft < [THRESHOLD]), and retrieve [USER_HOME_ADDRESS] from user profile.

2. Identify the contact's email from inbox results by matching against known identifiers (e.g., email domain pattern matching surname/organization); read the full email content once found.

3. Continue searching the inbox if the initial email list is truncated or the contact's email is not in the first page of results — expand or paginate the search until found.

4. Expand or paginate the property list if the initial results are truncated, to ensure all matching properties are retrieved.

5. Read the full contact email content to understand context before composing a reply.

6. Read user profile/detail endpoint to retrieve home address for ride booking origin.
Applies to: Multi-step agent tasks that require: (1) searching/filtering across multiple data sources (inbox, property listings, user profiles), (2) composing a reply message, (3) performing data mutations (saving/bookmarking items), and (4) scheduling/booking actions (ride orders). Applies to any task combining information retrieval with transactional write operations where the agent must transition from 'gather mode' to 'execute mode' without redundant re-verification.

[⚠️ Lesson from similar failed task]
Task: Cancel Thursday's dinner and replace it with a Wine Bar with Friends event, at the Restaurant, at the same time, with Nalani as an additional attendee. Inform the existing attendee via email to confirm that they are willing to attend. Let me know when these have been done. 

If that doesn't work for them, reschedule to accommodate any suggested changes and update my calendar accordingly. Make sure to reply to confirm, and also email Nalani about any changes.
Root cause: The task failed because the agent correctly identified and executed all atomic operations (cancel, create, search contacts) but did not complete the final confirmation loop — sending the email and reporting completion to the requester — likely due to context truncation or premature termination of the execution chain before reaching the notification step.
Known pitfall: When chaining calendar modification + email notification workflows, verify that EVERY leaf operation in the dependency graph completes (cancel event → create event → find contacts → send emails → monitor replies) before declaring done; a missing terminal step (email sent or status reported) invalidates the entire task even if all intermediate operations succeeded. Use explicit step-counting or a checklist pattern to ensure the final 'let me know when done' gate is reached.
What was attempted:
1. Determine the current date and identify the target day (e.g., Thursday) using a date/time operation.
2. Search the calendar for events on the target day matching the description pattern (e.g., '[EVENT_KEYWORD]') to locate the original event.
3. Extract the original event's metadata: event_id, start time, end time, venue, and list of current attendees.
4. Search contacts for the additional attendee ([ADDITIONAL_ATTENDEE_NAME]) and retrieve their contact details (email, phone).
5. Cancel the original event using its event_id.
6. Create a replacement event with: updated title ([NEW_EVENT_TITLE]), same start/end time, same or specified venue ([VENUE]), and the combined attendee list (original attendees + additional attendee).
7. Search contacts for each original attendee ([ORIGINAL_ATTENDEE_NAME]) and retrieve their full contact details (specifically email).
8. Send a confirmation/information email to each original attendee explaining the event change (from [OLD_EVENT_TITLE] to [NEW_EVENT_TITLE]), confirming they are willing to attend the updated event.
9. Monitor for incoming email replies from attendees regarding their willingness/availability.
10. If any attendee declines or requests changes: negotiate a new time, update the calendar event accordingly, reply to confirm to that attendee, and notify the additional attendee ([ADDITIONAL_ATTENDEE_NAME]) of any changes via email.
Applies to: Applies to any multi-step task involving: (1) modifying a scheduled event (cancel + recreate), (2) changing attendee lists, (3) notifying stakeholders via email, and (4) requiring explicit completion confirmation back to the requester. Concrete examples: rescheduling meetings with participant changes, converting meeting types (lunch→dinner, in-person→virtual), adding/removing attendees and notifying all parties, event logistics changes that require attendee re-confirmation.

Send an email to my friend in Leuven asking if they want to visit some cheap properties in the city with me tomorrow as I am taking the day off.
